annotations.label: Elliptic geometry (no parallel lines)

anchor.snippet: is an example of a geometry in which Euclid's parallel postulate does not hold

annotations.note: Mathlib has no synthetic/axiomatic elliptic geometry nor a parallel-postulate framework.

annotations.label: Single vs. double elliptic geometry

anchor.snippet: is sometimes referred to as single elliptic geometry

annotations.note: No notion of single vs. double elliptic geometry exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Triangle angle sum exceeds 180°

anchor.snippet: the sum of the interior angles of any triangle is always greater than 180°

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the Euclidean angle-sum (= π) but has no elliptic triangle theory giving sum > π.

annotations.label: Derivation from spherical geometry

anchor.snippet: Elliptic geometry may be derived from spherical geometry by identifying antipodal points

annotations.note: The antipodal map on the sphere exists, but the antipodal quotient yielding elliptic geometry is not formalized.

annotations.label: Absolute pole of a line

anchor.snippet: all perpendiculars to a given line intersect at a single point called the absolute pole

annotations.note: No concept of an absolute pole in elliptic geometry exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Absolute polar line and quadrant

anchor.snippet: Every point corresponds to an absolute polar line of which it is the absolute pole

annotations.note: No pole–polar correspondence for an elliptic plane is formalized.

annotations.label: Distance proportional to polar angle

anchor.snippet: The distance between a pair of points is proportional to the angle between their absolute polars

annotations.note: No elliptic distance/polar-angle relationship is formalized.

annotations.label: Elliptic vs. hyperbolic plane (naming)

anchor.snippet: a non-Euclidean plane is said to be elliptic or hyperbolic according as each of its lines contains no point at infinity or two points at infinity

annotations.note: Mathlib has no classification of non-Euclidean planes by points at infinity.

annotations.label: Elliptic plane

anchor.snippet: The elliptic plane is the real projective plane provided with a metric

annotations.note: The real projective plane exists as `Projectivization ℝ V`, but it carries no elliptic metric in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Projective axiom confirmed

anchor.snippet: the axiom of projective geometry, requiring all pairs of lines in a plane to intersect, is confirmed

annotations.note: Projectivization subspaces form a lattice, but the incidence theorem that two lines in a projective plane always meet is not formalized.

annotations.label: Elliptic distance

anchor.snippet: the elliptic distance between them is the measure of the angle POQ

annotations.note: No elliptic distance defined via the angle between representing lines is formalized.

annotations.label: Line segments cannot scale indefinitely

anchor.snippet: A line segment therefore cannot be scaled up indefinitely

annotations.note: No bounded-line-length property of elliptic geometry is formalized.

annotations.label: Euclid's postulates carried over

anchor.snippet: the first and fourth of Euclid's postulates, that there is a unique line between any two points and that all right angles are equal, hold in elliptic geometry

annotations.note: Mathlib does not encode Euclid's postulates as axioms to be checked in elliptic geometry.

annotations.label: Space is homogeneous and boundless

anchor.snippet: space is continuous, homogeneous, isotropic, and without boundaries

annotations.note: No homogeneity/isotropy property of elliptic space is formalized.

annotations.label: Triangle angle sum and 90-90-90 triangle

anchor.snippet: the sum of the interior angles of a triangle is greater than 180 degrees

annotations.note: No spherical/elliptic triangle with three right angles or angle excess is formalized.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem fails

anchor.snippet: The Pythagorean theorem fails in elliptic geometry

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the Euclidean Pythagorean theorem (`norm_add_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_of_inner_eq_zero`) but not its failure in elliptic geometry.

annotations.label: Circumference-to-area ratio

anchor.snippet: The ratio of a circle's circumference to its area is smaller than in Euclidean geometry

annotations.note: No elliptic circle circumference/area comparison is formalized.

annotations.label: Equipollence of directed arcs

anchor.snippet: directed arcs found on great circles are equipollent when they are of the same length, orientation, and great circle

annotations.note: No equipollence relation on directed arcs of great circles is formalized.

annotations.label: Euler's formula as a great circle

anchor.snippet: represents the great circle in the plane containing 1 and r

annotations.note: Euler's formula exists for ℂ but there is no quaternion exp `cos θ + r sin θ` interpreted as a great circle in elliptic space.

annotations.label: Arc length bounded by π

anchor.snippet: In elliptic space, arc length is less than π

annotations.note: No arc-length bound for elliptic space is formalized.

annotations.label: Modulus (norm) of z equals one

anchor.snippet: It is said that the modulus or norm of z is one

annotations.note: Quaternions and their norm exist (`Quaternion.normSq`), so the unit-norm condition is expressible, but no dedicated norm-one structure for elliptic geometry is defined.

annotations.label: Versor

anchor.snippet: Hamilton called a quaternion of norm one a versor

annotations.note: Unit quaternions are expressible via `Quaternion.normSq = 1`, but `versor` is not a named definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Versors forming an elliptic line

anchor.snippet: form an elliptic line

annotations.note: No notion of an elliptic line as a one-parameter family of versors is formalized.

annotations.label: Distance via scalar part of quaternion

anchor.snippet: since this is the formula for the scalar part of any quaternion

annotations.note: The scalar part `Quaternion.re` exists, but the elliptic distance formula in terms of it is not formalized.

annotations.label: Elliptic motion

anchor.snippet: An elliptic motion is described by the quaternion mapping

annotations.note: Quaternion multiplication exists, but the map `z ↦ a z b` as an elliptic motion is not formalized.

annotations.label: Elliptic motions preserve distance

anchor.snippet: Distances between points are the same as between image points of an elliptic motion

annotations.note: No elliptic motion or its distance-preservation property is formalized.

annotations.label: Conjugate versors give spatial rotation

anchor.snippet: the motion is a spatial rotation , and their vector part is the axis of rotation

annotations.note: Mathlib has a general Clifford spin-group conjugation action but no explicit unit-quaternion-conjugation = SO(3) rotation result.

annotations.label: Clifford translation (parataxy)

anchor.snippet: the elliptic motion is called a right Clifford translation , or a parataxy

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Clifford` refers to Clifford algebras, not Clifford translations/parataxies in elliptic space.

annotations.label: Clifford translations along elliptic lines

anchor.snippet: They are the right and left Clifford translations of u along an elliptic line through 1

annotations.note: No left/right Clifford translations along elliptic lines are formalized.

annotations.label: Elliptic space from S³

anchor.snippet: The elliptic space is formed from S 3 by identifying antipodal points

annotations.note: S³ exists as a sphere/manifold, but its antipodal quotient defining elliptic space is not formalized.

annotations.label: Clifford parallels and surfaces

anchor.snippet: Elliptic space has special structures called Clifford parallels and Clifford surfaces

annotations.note: No Clifford parallels or Clifford surfaces are formalized.

annotations.label: Cayley transform of versor points

anchor.snippet: The versor points of elliptic space are mapped by the Cayley transform

annotations.note: No Cayley transform of quaternions/versors exists in Mathlib (only Cayley–Hamilton and Cayley graphs).

annotations.label: Hyperspherical model points

anchor.snippet: The points of n -dimensional elliptic space are the pairs of unit vectors

annotations.note: The unit sphere exists, but the hyperspherical model (antipodal pairs of unit vectors as elliptic points) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Lines as great circles

anchor.snippet: Lines in this model are great circles

annotations.note: No definition of great circles as lines in a sphere model is formalized.

annotations.label: Projective model of elliptic geometry

anchor.snippet: the points of n -dimensional real projective space are used as points of the model

annotations.note: Real projective space exists as `Projectivization ℝ V`, but its use as a metric model of elliptic geometry is not formalized.

annotations.label: Points as lines through origin

anchor.snippet: The points of n -dimensional projective space can be identified with lines through the origin

annotations.note: `Projectivization.equivSubmodule` gives the equivalence between projective points and one-dimensional subspaces (lines through the origin) over any field.

annotations.label: Distance as angle between lines

anchor.snippet: the distance between two points is the angle between their corresponding lines

annotations.note: No elliptic distance defined as the angle between corresponding lines through the origin is formalized.

annotations.label: Non-orientability in even dimensions

anchor.snippet: for even dimensions, such as the plane, the geometry is non- orientable

annotations.note: No orientability/non-orientability result for projective space is formalized.

annotations.label: Stereographic model and chordal metric

anchor.snippet: can be obtained by means of stereographic projection

annotations.note: The stereographic projection of the sphere is formalized, but not as a chordal-metric model of elliptic geometry.

annotations.label: Bijective stereographic correspondence

anchor.snippet: represents the distance along a chord of the corresponding points on the hyperspherical model

annotations.note: `stereographic` is a partial homeomorphism (hence a bijection on its source), but its chordal-distance interpretation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Spherical geometry metric

anchor.snippet: We obtain a model of spherical geometry if we use the metric

annotations.note: No spherical-geometry metric defined by the stated formula is formalized.

annotations.label: Elliptic metric by antipodal identification

anchor.snippet: Elliptic geometry is obtained from this by identifying the antipodal points

annotations.note: No elliptic metric obtained via antipodal identification is formalized.

annotations.label: Relative consistency of elliptic geometry

anchor.snippet: if Euclidean geometry is self-consistent, so is spherical elliptic geometry

annotations.note: No relative-consistency metatheorem for elliptic vs. Euclidean geometry is formalized.

annotations.label: Tarski's completeness of Euclidean geometry

anchor.snippet: Tarski proved that elementary Euclidean geometry is complete

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize Tarski's axioms or the completeness/decidability of elementary Euclidean geometry.

annotations.label: Elliptic geometry consistent and complete

anchor.snippet: elementary elliptic geometry is also self-consistent and complete

annotations.note: No consistency/completeness metatheorem for elementary elliptic geometry is formalized.
